sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=4, col=37).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 6 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the top-right white square changed id=12 from purple to white! So the top-right indicators are color selectors - clicking one applies that color to the center object.
Current center state:
- id=12: now white (was purple) - the top center object
- id=13: purple (middle)
- id=14: white (bottom)
Left panel reference: white above purple
So now the center has: white, purple, white. The left panel has white above purple. I need to make the center match. The middle object (id=13) is purple and the bottom (id=14) is white. 
Looking at the left panel more carefully - it has white rect [3,3]-[7,12] and purple rect [8,3]-[12,12]. So it's a two-part pattern: white on top, purple on bottom.
The center currently has three objects. Maybe I need to make the top match white and the rest match purple? Or maybe the goal is different.
Let me look at what the top-right indicators show now. After clicking the white one, did it change? Let me also check if clicking the purple indicator will change the middle object.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The pattern seems to be:
- Click a color indicator in top-right to select a color
- That color gets applied to the center object
But which center object? It seems like it applied to id=12 (the top one). Maybe there's a selection mechanism - perhaps arrow keys select which object, and MOUSE on indicators applies color?
Wait, but arrow keys only affected the timer. Let me try clicking the purple indicator to see what happens, and also try clicking on the center objects themselves to "select" them first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click the purple indicator (top-right) to see what it does
# Purple square (id=7) at [3,42]-[5,44]
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])
print(f"MOUSE on top-right purple: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

if last_action_result.get('board_changed'):
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    for n in after['nodes']:
        bn = [x for x in before['nodes'] if x['id'] == n['id']]
        if bn:
            bn = bn[0]
            if bn['color'] != n['color'] or bn['pixels'] != n['pixels']:
                print(f"  id={n['id']}: color {bn['color']}->{n['color']}, px {bn['pixels']}->{n['pixels']}")

# Check current center state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} [{b[0]}]-[{b[2] if len(b)>2 else b[0]}]")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "One-time toggle on center objects", "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Tick down timer", "MOUSE on top-right indicator": "Applies that color to center object (white indicator→id=12 became white)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_right_indicators": "Color selectors - click to paint center objects"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_strip": "Timer bar at row 63", "top_right": "Color palette: white and purple squares"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1: left panel (white above purple) = reference/goal. Top-right has color selectors. Center has 3 objects (now white, purple, white). Clicking top-right indicators paints center objects.", "goal_model": "Match center to left panel pattern (white above purple)", "recent_findings": ["Clicking white indicator painted id=12 white", "Need to figure out which object gets painted and how to select"], "open_questions": ["Which center object gets painted?", "How to select different center objects?"], "current_plan": "Test purple indicator, then figure out selection mechanism", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["UP/DOWN only affect timer", "MOUSE on center: no effect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
MOUSE on top-right purple: board_changed=True
  id=12: color W->p, px 84->84
  id=12: color=p px=84 [[25, 26]]-[[31, 37]]
  id=13: color=p px=50 [[34, 27]]-[[38, 36]]
  id=14: color=W px=50 [[39, 27]]-[[43, 36]]
